text.NABRE: Indeed, though one be perfect among mortals, if Wisdom, who comes from you, be lacking, that one will count for nothing.

text.DRB: Thou hast chosen me king to thy people, and judge of thy sons and daughters:
text.KJV: Thou hast chosen me to be a king of thy people, and a Iudge of thy sons and daughters:
text.NABRE: You have chosen me king over your people and magistrate over your sons and daughters.

text.DRB: and badest me build a temple in thy holy mount, and an altar in the city of thy habitation, a similitude of thy holy tabernacle, which thou didst prepare from the beginning:
text.KJV: Thou hast commaunded me to build a Temple vpon thy holy mount, and an Altar in the city wherein thou dwellest, a resemblance of the holy Tabernacle which thou hast prepared from the beginning:
text.NABRE: You have bid me build a temple on your holy mountain and an altar in the city that is your dwelling place, a copy of the holy tabernacle which you had established from of old.

text.KJV: And wisedome was with thee: which knoweth thy workes, and was present when thou madest the world, and knew what was acceptable in thy sight, and right in thy Commaundements.
text.NABRE: Now with you is Wisdom, who knows your works and was present when you made the world; Who understands what is pleasing in your eyes and what is conformable with your commands.
text.NRSV: “With you is wisdom, she who knows your works and was present when you made the world; she understands what is pleasing in your sight and what is right according to your commandments.”
text.DRB: and thy wisdom with thee, which knew thy works, which then also was present when thou madest the round world, and knew what was pleasing to thine eyes, and what was direct in thy precepts.

text.KJV: O send her out of thy holy heauens, and from the Throne of thy glory, that being present shee may labour with mee, that I may know what is pleasing vnto thee.
text.NABRE: Send her forth from your holy heavens and from your glorious throne dispatch her That she may be with me and work with me, that I may know what is pleasing to you.
text.NRSV: “Send her forth from the holy heavens, and from the throne of your glory send her, that she may labor at my side, and that I may learn what is pleasing to you.”
text.DRB: Send her from thy holy heavens, and from the seat of thy greatness, that she may be with me, and may labour with me, that I may know what is acceptable with thee:
for she knoweth all things & understandeth, and shall conduct me in my works soberly, & shall keep me with her might.
text.KJV: For she knoweth and vnderstandeth all things, and shee shall leade me soberly in my doings, and preserue me in her power.
text.NABRE: For she knows and understands all things, and will guide me prudently in my affairs and safeguard me by her glory;

text.DRB: And my works shall be acceptable, and I shall govern thy people justly, and shall be worthy of the seats of my father.
text.KJV: So shall my workes be acceptable, and then shall I iudge thy people righteously, and be worthy to sit in my fathers seate.
text.NABRE: Thus my deeds will be acceptable, and I will judge your people justly and be worthy of my father’s throne.

text.KJV: For what man is hee that can know the counsell of God? or who can thinke what the will of the Lord is?
text.NABRE: For who knows God’s counsel, or who can conceive what the Lord intends?
text.NRSV: “For who can learn the counsel of God? Or who can discern what the Lord wills?”
text.DRB: For who of men is able to know the counsel of God? Or who can think what God will?
For the cogitations of mortal men be fearful, and our providences uncertain.
text.KJV: For the thoughts of mortall men are miserable, and our deuices are but vncertaine.
text.NABRE: For the deliberations of mortals are timid, and uncertain our plans.

text.KJV: For the corruptible body presseth downe the soule, and the earthy tabernacle weigheth downe the minde that museth vpon many things.
text.NABRE: For the corruptible body burdens the soul and the earthly tent weighs down the mind with its many concerns.
text.NRSV: “for a perishable body weighs down the soul, and this earthy tent burdens the thoughtful mind.”
text.DRB: For the body that is corrupted burdeneth the soul, and the earthly habitation presseth down the understanding that thinketh many things.
And we do hardly conjecture the things that are in the earth: and the things that are in sight, we find with labour. But the things that are in the heavens who shall search out?
text.KJV: And hardly doe we gesse aright at things that are vpon earth, and with labour doe wee find the things that are before vs: but the things that are in heauen, who hath searched out?
text.NABRE: Scarcely can we guess the things on earth, and only with difficulty grasp what is at hand; but things in heaven, who can search them out?

text.DRB: And thy sense who shall know, unless thou give wisdom, and send thy holy Spirit from on high:
text.KJV: And thy counsell who hath knowen, except thou giue wisedome, and send thy holy spirit from aboue?
text.NABRE: Or who can know your counsel, unless you give Wisdom and send your holy spirit from on high?

text.DRB: and so the paths of them that are on the earth, may be corrected, and men learn the things that please thee?
text.KJV: For so the wayes of them which liued on the earth were reformed, and men were taught the things that are pleasing vnto thee, and were saued through wisedome.
text.NABRE: Thus were the paths of those on earth made straight, and people learned what pleases you, and were saved by Wisdom.

text.DRB: For by wisdom they were healed, whosoever have pleased thee o Lord from the beginning.

text.KJV: She preserued the first formed father of the world that was created alone, and brought him out of his fall,
text.NABRE: Chapter 10 Wisdom Preserves Her Followers She preserved the first-formed father of the world when he alone had been created; And she raised him up from his fall,
text.NRSV: Wisdom protected the first-formed father of the world, when he alone had been created; she delivered him from his transgression,
text.DRB: She kept him, that was first made of God father of the world, when he was created alone,

text.KJV: And gaue him power to rule all things.
text.NABRE: and gave him power to rule all things.
text.NRSV: and gave him strength to rule all things.
text.DRB: and she brought him out of his sin, and gave him power to contain all things.
After the unjust departed in his anger from her, by the fury of brother's manslaughter he perished.
text.KJV: But when the vnrighteous went away from her in his anger, he perished also in the fury wherwith he murdered his brother.
text.NABRE: But when an unrighteous man withdrew from her in his anger, he perished through his fratricidal wrath.

text.KJV: For whose cause the earth being drowned with the flood, Wisedome againe preserued it, & directed the course of the righteous, in a piece of wood, of small value.
text.NABRE: When on his account the earth was flooded, Wisdom again saved it, piloting the righteous man on frailest wood.
text.NRSV: When the earth was flooded because of him, wisdom again saved it, steering the righteous man by a paltry piece of wood.
text.DRB: For whose cause, when water destroyed the earth, wisdom healed it again, governing the just by contemptible wood.

text.KJV: Moreouer, the nations in their wicked conspiracie being confounded, she found out the righteous, and preserued him blamelesse vnto God, and kept him strong against his tender compassion towards his sonne.
text.NABRE: She, when the nations were sunk in universal wickedness, knew the righteous man, kept him blameless before God, and preserved him resolute against pity for his child.
text.NRSV: Wisdom also, when the nations in wicked agreement had been confounded, recognized the righteous man and kept him blameless before God, and held him strong in the face of his compassion for his child.
text.DRB: She even in the consent of wickedness, when the nations had confederated themselves, knew the just, and preserved him without blame to God, and in his son's mercy kept the strong.

text.KJV: When the vngodly perished, shee deliuered the righteous man, who fled from the fire which fell downe vpon the fiue cities.
text.NABRE: She rescued a righteous man from among the wicked who were being destroyed, when he fled as fire descended upon the Pentapolis—
text.NRSV: Wisdom rescued a righteous man when the ungodly were perishing; he escaped the fire that descended on the Five Cities.
text.DRB: She delivered the just, flying from the impious that perished, when the fire came down upon Pentapolis:
to whom for a witness of their wickedness the desert land standeth smoking, and trees having fruits at uncertain season; and the memory of an incredulous soul a standing pillar of salt.
text.KJV: Of whose wickednesse euen to this day the waste land that smoketh, is a testimonie, and plants bearing fruite that neuer come to ripenesse: and a standing pillar of salt is a monument of an vnbeleeuing soule.
text.NABRE: Where as a testimony to its wickedness, even yet there remain a smoking desert, Plants bearing fruit that never ripens, and the tomb of a disbelieving soul, a standing pillar of salt.

text.DRB: For pretermitting wisdom they did not only slip in this, that they were ignorant of good things, but they left also unto men a memory of their foolishness, that in those things, in which they sinned in, they could not be hid neither.
text.KJV: For regarding not wisedome, they gate not only this hurt, that they knew not the things which were good: but also left behind them to the world a memoriall of their foolishnes: so that in the things wherein they offended, they could not so much as be hid.
text.NABRE: For those who forsook Wisdom not only were deprived of knowledge of the good, But also left the world a memorial of their folly, so that they could not even be hidden in their fall.

text.DRB: But wisdom hath delivered them that observe her from sorrows.
text.KJV: But Wisedome deliuered from paine those that attended vpon her.
text.NABRE: But Wisdom rescued from tribulations those who served her.

text.KJV: When the righteous fled from his brothers wrath, she guided him in right paths: shewed him the kingdome of God: and gaue him knowledge of holy things, made him rich in his trauailes, and multiplied the fruit of his labours.
text.NABRE: She, when a righteous man fled from his brother’s anger, guided him in right ways, Showed him the kingdom of God and gave him knowledge of holy things; She prospered him in his labors and made abundant the fruit of his works,
text.NRSV: When a righteous man fled from his brother’s wrath, she guided him on straight paths; she showed him the kingdom of God, and gave him knowledge of holy things.
text.DRB: And the just flying his brother's wrath, she conducted by the right ways, and shewed him the kingdom of God, and gave him the knowledge of the holy, did honest him in labours, and accomplished his labours.
In the fraud of the circumventers of him she was present with him, and made him honourable.
text.KJV: In the couetousnesse of such as oppressed him, she stood by him, and made him rich.
text.NABRE: Stood by him against the greed of his defrauders, and enriched him;

text.DRB: She kept him from the enemies, and from seducers she defended him, and gave him a strong fight, that he might overcome, and know that wisdom is mightier than all.
text.KJV: She defended him from his enemies, and kept him safe from those that lay in wait, and in a sore conflict she gaue him the victory, that he might knowe that godlinesse is stronger then all.
text.NABRE: She preserved him from foes, and secured him against ambush, And she gave him the prize for his hard struggle that he might know that devotion to God is mightier than all else.

text.DRB: She forsook not the just being sold, but delivered him from sinners: and she went down with him into the pit,
text.KJV: When the righteous was solde, she forsooke him not, but deliuered him from sinne: she went downe with him into the pit,
text.NABRE: She did not abandon a righteous man when he was sold, but rescued him from sin.

text.DRB: and in bands left him not, till she brought him the sceptre of a Kingdom, and might against them that oppressed him: and shewed them to be liars, that spotted him, and gave him eternal glory.
text.KJV: And left him not in bonds till she brought him the scepter of the kingdom and power against those that oppressed him: as for them that had accused him, she shewed them to be liers, and gaue them perpetuall glory.
text.NABRE: She went down with him into the dungeon, and did not desert him in his bonds, Until she brought him the scepter of royalty and authority over his oppressors, Proved false those who had defamed him, and gave him eternal glory.

text.DRB: The just people, and seed without blame she delivered from the nations, that oppressed them.
text.KJV: She deliuered the righteous people, and blamelesse seed from the nation that oppressed them.
text.NABRE: The holy people and their blameless descendants—it was she who rescued them from the nation that oppressed them.

text.DRB: She entered into the soul of the servant of God, and stood against dreadful kings in wonders and signs.
text.KJV: She entred into the soule of the seruant of the Lord, and withstood dreadfull kings in wonders and signes,
text.NABRE: She entered the soul of the Lord’s servant, and withstood fearsome kings with signs and wonders;

text.DRB: And she rendered to the just the hope of their labours, and conducted them in a marvelous way: and she was unto them for a cover in the day, and for the light of stars by night:
text.KJV: Rendred to the righteous a reward of their labours, guided them in a marueilous way, and was vnto them for a couer by day, and a light of starres in the night season:
text.NABRE: she gave the holy ones the reward of their labors, Conducted them by a wondrous road, became a shelter for them by day a starry flame by night.

text.DRB: and she transported them through the Red sea, and carried them over through a great water.
text.KJV: Brought them through the red sea, and led them thorow much water.
text.NABRE: She took them across the Red Sea and brought them through the deep waters.

text.DRB: But their enemies she drowned in the sea, and from the depth of hell she brought them out. Therefore the just took the spoils of the impious,
text.KJV: But she drowned their enemies, and cast them vp out of the bottome of the deepe.
text.NABRE: Their enemies she overwhelmed, and churned them up from the bottom of the depths.

text.DRB: and they sang thy holy name o Lord, and thy victorious hand they praised together:
text.KJV: Therefore the righteous spoiled the vngodly, & praised thy holy Name, O Lord, and magnified with one accord thine hand that fought for them.
text.NABRE: Therefore the righteous despoiled the wicked; and they sang of your holy name, Lord, and praised in unison your conquering hand,

text.DRB: because wisdom hath opened the mouth of the dumb, and the tongues of infants she hath made eloquent.
text.KJV: For wisedome opened the mouth of the dumbe, and made the tongues of them that cannot speake, eloquent.
text.NABRE: Because Wisdom opened the mouths of the mute, and gave ready speech to infants.

text.KJV: She prospered their works in the hand of the holy Prophet.
text.NABRE: Chapter 11 She prospered their affairs through the holy prophet.
text.NRSV: Wisdom prospered their works by the hand of a holy prophet.
text.DRB: She directed their works in the hands of a holy Prophet.

text.KJV: They went thorough the wildernesse that was not inhabited, and pitched tents in places where there lay no way.
text.NABRE: III. Special Providence of God During the Exodus Introduction They journeyed through the uninhabited desert, and in lonely places they pitched their tents;
text.NRSV: They journeyed through an uninhabited wilderness, and pitched their tents in untrodden places.
text.DRB: They made a journey through the deserts, that were not inhabited: and in desert places they pitched cottages.
They stood against the adversaries, and revenged themselves of the enemies.
text.KJV: They stood against their enemies, and were auenged of their aduersaries.
text.NABRE: they withstood enemies and warded off their foes.

text.DRB: They thirsted, and invocated thee, and water was given them out of a most high rock, and quenching of their thirst out of the hard stone.
text.KJV: When they were thirsty they called vpon thee, and water was giuen them out of the flinty rocke, and their thirst was quenched out of the hard stone.
text.NABRE: When they thirsted, they called upon you, and water was given them from the sheer rock, a quenching of their thirst from the hard stone.

text.DRB: For by the things whereby their enemies suffered punishment, for defect of their drink, and therein, when the children of Israel abounded, they did rejoice:
text.KJV: For by what things their enemies were punished, by the same they in their neede were benefited.
text.NABRE: For by the things through which their foes were punished they in their need were benefited.

text.DRB: by these things, when others lacked the same, it went well with them.
text.KJV: For in stead of a fountaine of a perpetuall running riuer, troubled with foule blood,
text.NABRE: First Example: Water Punishes the Egyptians and Benefits the Israelites Instead of a river’s perennial source, troubled with impure blood

text.DRB: For in stead of the fountain of an everlasting river, thou gavest man's blood to the unjust.
text.KJV: For a manifest reproofe of that commandement, whereby the infants were slaine, thou gauest vnto them abundance of water by a meanes which they hoped not for,
text.NABRE: as a rebuke to the decree for the slaying of infants, You gave them abundant water beyond their hope,

text.DRB: Who when they were diminished in the destruction of the murdered infants, thou gavest them aboundant water unlooked for:
text.KJV: Declaring by that thirst then, how thou hadst punished their aduersaries.
text.NABRE: after you had shown by the thirst they experienced how you punished their adversaries.

text.DRB: shewing by the thirst, that then was, how thou didst exalt thine, & didst kill their adversaries.
text.KJV: For when they were tryed, albeit but in mercy chastised, they knew how the vngodly were iudged in wrath and tormented thirsting in another maner then the Iust.
text.NABRE: For when they had been tried, though only mildly chastised, they recognized how the wicked, condemned in anger, were being tormented.

text.DRB: For when they were tempted, and indeed with mercy taking discipline, they knew how the impious being judged with wrath did suffer torments.
text.KJV: For these thou didst admonish, and trie as a father: but the other as a seuere king thou didst condemne and punish.
text.NABRE: You tested your own people, admonishing them as a father; but as a stern king you probed and condemned the wicked.

text.DRB: These certes admonishing as a father thou didst prove: but them examining as a hard king thou didst condemn.
text.KJV: Whether they were absent, or present, they were vexed alike.
text.NABRE: Those near and far were equally afflicted:

text.DRB: For the absent and the present were tormented alike.
text.KJV: For a double griefe came vpon them, and a groaning for the remembrance of things past.
text.NABRE: for a twofold grief took hold of them and a groaning at the remembrance of the ones who had departed.

text.DRB: For double tediousness had taken them, and sighing with the memory of good things past.
text.KJV: For when they heard by their owne punishments the other to be benefited, they had some feeling of the Lord.
text.NABRE: For when they heard that the cause of their own torments was a benefit to these others, they recognized the Lord.

text.DRB: For when they understood by their punishment that it went well with them, they remembered our Lord, marveling at the end of the event.
text.KJV: For whom they reiected with scorne when hee was long before throwen out at the casting forth of the infants, him in the end, when they saw what came to passe, they admired.
text.NABRE: For though they had mocked and rejected him who had been cast out and abandoned long ago, in the final outcome, they marveled at him, since their thirst proved unlike that of the righteous.

text.DRB: For whom before they derided, being cast forth in that wicked laying out to perish, him they marveled at in the end of the event: not thirsting in like manner to the just.
text.KJV: But for the foolish deuises of their wickednesse, wherewith being deceiued, they worshipped serpents voyd of reason, and vile beasts: thou didst send a multitude of vnreasonable beasts vpon them for vengeance,
text.NABRE: Second Example: Animals Punish the Egyptians and Benefit the Israelites In return for their senseless, wicked thoughts, which misled them into worshiping dumb serpents and worthless insects, You sent upon them swarms of dumb creatures for vengeance;

text.DRB: But for senseless cogitations of their iniquity, for that some erring did worship dumb serpents and superfluous beasts, thou didst send upon them a multitude of dumb beasts for revenge:
text.KJV: That they might knowe that wherewithall a man sinneth, by the same also shall he be punished.
text.NABRE: that they might recognize that one is punished by the very things through which one sins.

text.DRB: that they might know that by what things a man sinneth, by the same also he is tormented.
text.KJV: For thy Almighty hand that made the world of matter without forme, wanted not meanes to send among them a multitude of Beares, or fierce Lyons,
text.NABRE: Digression on God’s Mercy For not without means was your almighty hand, that had fashioned the universe from formless matter, to send upon them many bears or fierce lions,

text.DRB: For thine omnipotent hand, which made the world of invisible matter, was not unable to send upon them a multitude of bears, or fierce lions,
text.KJV: Or vnknowen wild beasts full of rage newly created, breathing out either a fiery vapour, or filthy sents of scattered smoake, or shooting horrible sparkles out of their eyes:
text.NABRE: Or newly created, wrathful, unknown beasts breathing forth fiery breath, Or pouring out roaring smoke, or flashing terrible sparks from their eyes.

text.DRB: or unknown beasts full of anger of a new kind; or breathing the vapour of fires, or casting forth the savour of smoke, or shooting horrible sparks from their eyes:
text.KJV: Whereof not onely the harme might dispatch them at once: but also the terrible sight vtterly destroy them.
text.NABRE: Not only could these attack and completely destroy them; even their frightful appearance itself could slay.

text.DRB: of which not only their hurt was able to destroy them, but also their sight to kill them for fear.
text.KJV: Yea and without these might they haue fallen downe with one blast, being persecuted of vengeance, and scattered abroad thorough the breath of thy power, but thou hast ordered all things in measure, and number, and weight.
text.NABRE: Even without these, they could have been killed at a single blast, pursued by justice and winnowed by your mighty spirit. But you have disposed all things by measure and number and weight.

text.KJV: For thou canst shew thy great strength at all times when thou wilt, and who may withstand the power of thine arme?
text.NABRE: For great strength is always present with you; who can resist the might of your arm?
text.NRSV: For it is always in your power to show great strength, and who can withstand the might of your arm?
text.DRB: Yea and without these with one spirit they might have been slain suffering persecution of their own facts, and dispersed by the spirit of thy power: but thou hast disposed all things in measure, and number, and weight.

text.KJV: For the whole world before thee is as a litle graine of the ballance, yea as a drop of the morning dew that falleth downe vpon the earth.
text.NABRE: Indeed, before you the whole universe is like a grain from a balance, or a drop of morning dew come down upon the earth.
text.NRSV: Because the whole world before you is like a speck that tips the scales, and like a drop of morning dew that falls on the ground.
text.DRB: For, to be of great force rested always in thee only: & who shall resist the power of thine arm?

text.KJV: But thou hast mercy vpon all: for thou canst doe all things, and winkest at the sinnes of men: because they should amend.
text.NABRE: But you have mercy on all, because you can do all things; and you overlook sins for the sake of repentance.
text.NRSV: But you are merciful to all, for you can do all things, and you overlook people’s sins, so that they may repent.
text.DRB: Because as the least weight of the balance, so is the round world before thee, and as a drop of the dew before day, that falleth upon the earth.

text.KJV: For thou louest all the things that are, and abhorrest nothing which thou hast made: for neuer wouldest thou haue made any thing, if thou hadst hated it.
text.NABRE: For you love all things that are and loathe nothing that you have made; for you would not fashion what you hate.
text.NRSV: For you love all things that exist, and detest none of the things that you have made, for you would not have made anything if you had hated it.
text.DRB: But thou hast mercy on all, because thou canst do all things, and dissemblest the sins of men for repentance.
For thou lovest all things that are, and hatest nothing of those which thou hast made: for thou didst not ordain or make any thing hating it.
text.KJV: And how could any thing haue endured if it had not beene thy will? or beene preserued, if not called by thee?
text.NABRE: How could a thing remain, unless you willed it; or be preserved, had it not been called forth by you?

text.KJV: But thou sparest all: for they are thine, O Lord, thou louer of soules.
text.NABRE: But you spare all things, because they are yours, O Ruler and Lover of souls, for your imperishable spirit is in all things!
text.NRSV: You spare all things, for they are yours, O Lord, you who love the living.
text.DRB: And how could any thing continue, unless thou wouldest? Or be preserved which was not called of thee?
But thou sparest all: because they are thine o Lord, which lovest souls.

text.KJV: For thine vncorruptible spirit is in all things.
text.NRSV: For your immortal spirit is in all things.
text.DRB: Ohow good and sweet is thy spirit o Lord in all!

text.KJV: Therefore chastnest thou them by little, and little, that offend, and warnest them by putting them in remembrance, wherin they haue offended, that leauing their wickednesse they may beleeue on thee O Lord.
text.NRSV: Therefore you correct little by little those who trespass, and you remind and warn them of the things through which they sin, so that they may be freed from wickedness and put their trust in you, O Lord.
text.DRB: And therefore those that err, by parts thou doest chastise: and doest admonish, and speak to them, concerning the things wherein they sin: that leaving naughtiness, they may believe in thee o Lord.
For those old inhabitants of thy holy land, whom thou didst abhor,
text.KJV: For it was thy will to destroy by the handes of our fathers, both those old inhabitants of thy holy land,

text.DRB: because they did works odious to thee by sorceries, and unjust sacrifices,
text.KJV: Whom thou hatedst for doing most odious workes of witchcrafts, and wicked sacrifices;

text.DRB: and the murderers of their own children without mercy, and eaters of men's bowels, and devourers of blood from the midst of thy sacrament,
text.KJV: And also those mercilesse murderers of children, & deuourers of mans flesh, and the feasts of blood;

text.DRB: and the parents authors of aidless souls, thou wouldst destroy by the hands of our parents,
text.KJV: With their Priests out of the midst of their idolatrous crew, and the parents that killed with their owne hands, soules destitute of helpe:

text.DRB: that they might receive a peregrination worthy of the children of God, which is a land of all most dear to thee.
text.KJV: That the land which thou esteemedst aboue all other, might receiue a worthy colonie of Gods children.

text.DRB: But them also as men thou didst spare, and didst send forerunners of thine host, wasps, that by little and little they might destroy them.
text.KJV: Neuerthelesse, euen those thou sparedst as men, and didst send waspes forerunners of thine hoste, to destroy them by little and little.

text.DRB: Not because thou wast unable in battle to subdue the impious to the just, or with cruel beasts, or with a sharp word to destroy them together:
text.KJV: Not that thou wast vnable to bring the vngodly vnder the hand of the righteous in battell, or to destroy them at once with cruel beastes, or with one rough word:

But executing thy iudgements vpon them by little and little, thou gauest them place of repentance, not being ignorant that they were a naughtie generation, and that their malice, was bred in them, and that their cogitation would neuer be changed.
text.NRSV: But judging them little by little you gave them an opportunity to repent, though you were not unaware that their origin was evil and their wickedness inborn.
text.DRB: but judging by parts thou gavest place of repentance, being not ignorant, that the nation of them is wicked, and their malice natural & that their cogitation could not be changed for ever.
For it was a cursed seed from the beginning: neither fearing any, didst thou give pardon to their sins.
text.KJV: For it was a cursed seed, from the beginning, neither didst thou for feare of any man giue them pardon for those things wherein they sinned.

text.DRB: For who shall say to thee: what hast thou done? Or who shall stand against thy judgement? Or who in thy sight shall come revenger of the wicked men? Or who shall impute it to thee, if the nations perish, which thou hast made?
text.KJV: For who shall say, What hast thou done? or who shall withstand thy iudgement, or who shall accuse thee for the nations that perish whom thou hast made? or who shall come to stand against thee, to be reuenged for the vnrighteous men?
For neither is there any God but thou, that careth for all, to whom thou mightest shew that thy iudgement is not vnright.
text.NRSV: For neither is there any god besides you, whose care is for all people, to whom you should prove that you have not judged unjustly.
text.DRB: For there is no other God but thou, who hast care of all, that thou mayst shew that thou doest not give judgement unjustly.
Neither king, nor tyrant in thy sight shall inquire of them, whom thou hast destroyed.
text.KJV: Neither shall king or tyrant bee able to set his face against thee, for any whom thou hast punished.
For so much then as thou art righteous thy selfe, thou orderest all things righteously: thinking it not agreeable with thy power to condemne him þ hath not deserued to be punished.
text.NRSV: But being righteous, you rule all things righteously, deeming it alien to your power to condemn anyone who does not deserve to be punished.
text.DRB: For so much then as thou art just, thou doest dispose all things justly: thou also esteemest it disagreeable from thy power to condemn him, who ought not to be punished.

text.KJV: For thy power is the beginning of righteousnesse, and because thou art the Lord of all, it maketh thee to be gracious vnto all.
text.NRSV: For your strength is the source of righteousness, and your sovereignty over all causes you to spare all.
text.DRB: For thy power is the beginning of justice: and for this that thou art Lord of all, thou makest thyself to spare all.
For thou shewest power, which art not thought to be absolute in power, and thou convincest the boldness of them, that know thee not.
text.KJV: For when men will not beleeue, that thou art of a full power, thou shewest thy strength, and among them that know it, thou makest their boldnesse manifest.

text.DRB: But thou dominatour of power, judgest with tranquility, and with great reverence disposest of us: for it is in thy power when thou wilt, to be able.
text.KJV: But thou, mastering thy power, iudgest with equitie, and orderest vs with great fauour: for thou mayest vse power when thou wilt.

text.DRB: And thou hast taught thy people by such works, that they must be just and gentle, and hast made thy children of good hope: because judging thou givest in sins place of repentance.
text.KJV: But by such workes hast thou taught thy people, that the iust man should be mercifull, and hast made thy children to be of a good hope, that thou giuest repentance for sinnes.

text.DRB: For if thou didst punish the enemies of thy servants, and that deserved to die, with so great consideration, giving time and place, whereby they might be changed from their wickedness:
text.KJV: For if thou didst punish the enemies of thy children, and the condemned to death with such deliberation, giuing them time and place, wherby they might be deliuered from their malice.

text.DRB: with what diligence hast thou judged thy children, to whose parents thou gavest oaths and covenants of good promises?
text.KJV: With how great circumspection diddest thou iudge thine owne sonnes, vnto whose fathers thou hast sworne, and made couenants of good promises?

text.DRB: Therefore when thou givest us discipline, thou scourgest our enemies very many ways, that judging we may think upon thy goodness: and when we are judged, we may hope for thy mercy.
text.KJV: Therefore whereas thou doest chasten vs, thou scourgest our enemies a thousand times more, to the intent that when wee iudge, wee should carefully thinke of thy goodnesse, and when we our selues are iudged, wee should looke for mercy.

text.DRB: Wherefore to them also, which in their life have lived foolishly & unjustly, thou hast given great torments by the same things, which they did worship.
text.KJV: Wherefore, whereas men haue liued dissolutely and vnrighteously, thou hast tormented them with their owne abominations.

text.DRB: For they wandered long in the way of error, esteeming for gods those things, that in beasts are superfluous, living after the manner of senseless infants.
text.KJV: For they went astray very farre in the wayes of errour, & held them for gods (which euen amongst the beasts of their enemies were despised) being deceiued as children of no vnderstanding.

text.DRB: For this cause thou hast given judgement on them as on senseless children to be in derision.
text.KJV: Therefore vnto them, as to children without the vse of reason, thou didst send a iudgement to mocke them.

text.DRB: But they that were not amended by scorns and reprehensions, have tried the worthy judgment of God.
text.KJV: But they that would not bee refourmed by that correction wherein he dallied with them, shall feele a iudgement worthy of God.

text.DRB: For in what things they suffering took indignation, by those whom they thought gods, when seeing they were destroyed in them, him, whom in time past they denied that they knew, they acknowledged the true God: for the which cause the end also of their condemnation shall come upon them.
text.KJV: For looke, for what things they grudged when they were punished, (that is) for them whom they thought to be gods,  being punished in them; when they saw it, they acknowledged him to be the true God, whome before they denyed to know: and therefore came extreme damnation vpon them.

Surely vaine are all men by nature, who are ignorant of God, and could not out of the good things that are seene, know him that is: neither by considering the workes, did they acknowledge the worke-master;
text.NABRE: Chapter 13 Digression on False Worship A. Nature Worship Foolish by nature were all who were in ignorance of God, and who from the good things seen did not succeed in knowing the one who is, and from studying the works did not discern the artisan;
text.NRSV: For all people who were ignorant of God were foolish by nature; and they were unable from the good things that are seen to know the one who exists, nor did they recognize the artisan while paying heed to his works;
text.DRB: But all men be vain, in whom there is not the knowledge of God: and of these good things which are seen they could not understand him that is, neither attending to the works have they agnised who was the workman:

text.KJV: But deemed either fire, or wind, or the swift aire, or the circle of the stars, or the violent water, or the lights of heauen to be the gods which gouerne the world:
text.NABRE: Instead either fire, or wind, or the swift air, or the circuit of the stars, or the mighty water, or the luminaries of heaven, the governors of the world, they considered gods.
text.NRSV: but they supposed that either fire or wind or swift air, or the circle of the stars, or turbulent water, or the luminaries of heaven were the gods that rule the world.
text.DRB: but either the fire, or the wind, or the swift air, or a circle of stars, or exceeding much water, or the sun and moon, they thought to be gods, rulers of the world.

text.KJV: With whose beautie, if they being delighted, tooke them to be gods: let them know how much better the Lord of them is; for the first Author of beautie hath created them.
text.NABRE: Now if out of joy in their beauty they thought them gods, let them know how far more excellent is the Lord than these; for the original source of beauty fashioned them.
text.NRSV: If through delight in the beauty of these things people assumed them to be gods, let them know how much better than these is their Lord, for the author of beauty created them.
text.DRB: With whose beauty if being delighted they thought them gods: let them know how much the Lord of them is more beautiful than they. For the author of beauty made all those things.
Or if they marveled at their virtue, & operations, let them understand by them, that he which made these, is stronger than they:
text.KJV: But if they were astonished at their power and vertue, let them vnderstand by them, how much mightier he is that made them.
text.NABRE: Or if they were struck by their might and energy, let them realize from these things how much more powerful is the one who made them.

text.KJV: For by the greatnesse and beautie of the creatures, proportionably the Maker of them is seene.
text.NABRE: For from the greatness and the beauty of created things their original author, by analogy, is seen.
text.NRSV: For from the greatness and beauty of created things comes a corresponding perception of their Creator.
text.DRB: for by the greatness of the beauty, and of the creature, the Creator of them may be seen, to be known thereby.
But notwithstanding there is yet in these less complaint. For they also perhaps err, seeking God, and desirous to find him.
text.KJV: But yet for this they are the lesse to bee blamed: for they peraduenture erre seeking God, and desirous to finde him.
text.NABRE: But yet, for these the blame is less; For they have gone astray perhaps, though they seek God and wish to find him.

text.DRB: For whereas they converse in his works, they inquire: & they are persuaded that the things be good which are seen.
text.KJV: For being conuersant in his workes, they search him diligently, and beleeue their sight: because the things are beautifull that are seene.
text.NABRE: For they search busily among his works, but are distracted by what they see, because the things seen are fair.

text.DRB: But again neither ought these to be pardoned.
text.KJV: Howbeit, neither are they to bee pardoned.
text.NABRE: But again, not even these are pardonable.

text.DRB: For if they could know so much, that they were able to estimate the world: how did they not more easily find the Lord thereof?
text.KJV: For if they were able to know so much, that they could aime at the world; how did they not sooner finde out the Lord thereof?
text.NABRE: For if they so far succeeded in knowledge that they could speculate about the world, how did they not more quickly find its Lord?

text.KJV: But miserable are they, and in dead things is their hope, who called them gods which are the workes of mens hands, golde and siluer, to shewe arte in, and resemblances of beasts, or a stone good for nothing, the worke of an ancient hand.
text.NABRE: B. Idolatry But wretched are they, and in dead things are their hopes, who termed gods things made by human hands: Gold and silver, the product of art, and images of beasts, or useless stone, the work of an ancient hand.
text.NRSV: But miserable, with their hopes set on dead things, are those who give the name “gods” to the works of human hands, gold and silver fashioned with skill.
text.DRB: But they are unhappy, & their hope is among the dead, who have called the works of men's hands gods, gold & silver, the invention of art, and the similitudes of beasts, or an unprofitable stone the work of an old hand.

Or if an artificer a carpenter, cut straight timber out of the wood, & pare off all the bark thereof cunningly, and using his art diligently frameth a vessel profitable for the common use of this life,
text.KJV: Now a carpenter that felleth timber, after hee hath sawen downe a tree meet for the purpose, and taken off all the barke skilfully round about, and hath wrought it handsomely, & made a vessell thereof fit for the seruice of mans life:
text.NABRE: The Carpenter and Wooden Idols A carpenter may cut down a suitable tree and skillfully scrape off all its bark, And deftly plying his art produce something fit for daily use,

text.DRB: and useth the chips of that work to dress his meat:
text.KJV: And after spending the refuse of his worke to dresse his meat, hath filled himselfe:
text.NABRE: And use the scraps from his handiwork in preparing his food, and have his fill;

text.DRB: and maketh that which is left thereof, which is for no uses, but being a crooked piece of wood, and full of knobs, carveth it diligently in the hollowness thereof, and by the skill of his art fashioneth it, and maketh it like to the image of a man,
text.KJV: And taking the very refuse among those which serued to no vse (being a crooked piece of wood, and ful of knots) hath carued it diligently when hee had nothing else to doe, and formed it by the skill of his vnderstanding, and fashioned it to the image of a man:
text.NABRE: Then the good-for-nothing refuse from these remnants, crooked wood grown full of knots, he takes and carves to occupy his spare time. This wood he models with mindless skill, and patterns it on the image of a human being

text.DRB: or compareth it to some beast, straking it over with red, and with painting making the colour thereof ruddy, and layeth a colour over every spot that is in it:
text.KJV: Or made it like some vile beast, laying it ouer with vermilion, and with paint, colouring it red, and couering euery spot therein:
text.NABRE: or makes it resemble some worthless beast. When he has daubed it with red and crimsoned its surface with red stain, and daubed over every blemish in it,

text.DRB: and maketh a worthy habitation for it, and setting it in a wall, and fastening it with iron
text.KJV: And when he had made a conuenient roume for it, set it in a wall, and made it fast with yron:
text.NABRE: He makes a fitting shrine for it and puts it on the wall, fastening it with a nail.

text.DRB: lest perhaps it fall, providing for it, knowing that it cannot help itself: for it is an image, and it needeth help.
text.KJV: For he prouided for it, that it might not fall: knowing that it was vnable to helpe it selfe, (for it is an image and hath neede of helpe:)
text.NABRE: Thus he provides for it lest it fall down, knowing that it cannot help itself; for, truly, it is an image and needs help.

text.DRB: And concerning his substance, & his children, & for marriage, making a vow he seeketh to it. He is not ashamed to speak with him, that is without soul:
text.KJV: Then maketh hee prayer for his goods, for his wife and children, and is not ashamed to speake to that which hath no life.
text.NABRE: But when he prays about his goods or marriage or children, he is not ashamed to address the thing without a soul. For vigor he invokes the powerless;

text.DRB: and for health certes he beseecheth the weak, and for life asketh the dead, and for help invocateth him that is unprofitable:
text.KJV: For health, hee calleth vpon that which is weake: for life, prayeth to that which is dead: for aide, humbly beseecheth that which hath least meanes to helpe: and for a good iourney, hee asketh of that which cannot set a foot forward:
text.NABRE: for life he entreats the dead; For aid he beseeches the wholly incompetent; for travel, something that cannot even walk;

text.DRB: and for a journey asketh him, that cannot walk: and for getting, and for working, and for the event of all things he asketh him, that in all is unprofitable.
text.KJV: And for gaining and getting, and for good successe of his hands, asketh abilitie to doe, of him that is most vnable to doe any thing.
text.NABRE: For profit in business and success with his hands he asks power of a thing with hands utterly powerless.

text.DRB: Again another thinking to sail, and beginning to make a journey through the fierce waves, invocateth wood more frail than the wood that carrieth him.
text.KJV: Againe, one preparing himselfe to saile, and about to passe through the raging waues, calleth vpon a piece of wood more rotten then the vesell that carieth him.
text.NABRE: Chapter 14 Again, one preparing for a voyage and about to traverse the wild waves cries out to wood more unsound than the boat that bears him.

text.DRB: For covetousness of getting invented it, and the craftsman by his wisdom framed it.
text.KJV: For verely desire of gaine deuised that, and the workeman built it by his skill:
text.NABRE: For the urge for profits devised this latter, and Wisdom the artisan produced it.

text.DRB: But thy providence, o Father, doth govern: because thou hast given a way even in the sea, and among the waves a most sure path,
text.KJV: But thy prouidence, O Father, gouerneth it: for thou hast made a way in the Sea, and a safe path in the waues:
text.NABRE: But your providence, O Father! guides it, for you have furnished even in the sea a road, and through the waves a steady path,

text.DRB: shewing that thou art able to save out of all things, yea if a man go to the sea without art.
text.KJV: Shewing that thou canst saue from all danger: yea though a man went to Sea without arte.
text.NABRE: Showing that you can save from any danger, so that even one without skill may embark.

text.DRB: But that thy works might not be void of wisdom: for this cause also men commit their lives even to a little wood, and passing over the sea are delivered by ship.
text.KJV: Neuerthelesse thou wouldest not that the works of thy wisedome should be idle, and therefore doe men commit their liues to a small piece of wood, and passing the rough sea in a weake vessell, are saued.
text.NABRE: But you will that the products of your Wisdom be not idle; therefore people trust their lives even to most frail wood, and were safe crossing the waves on a raft.

text.DRB: But from the beginning also when the proud giants perished, the hope of the world flying to a ship, rendered to the world seed of nativity, which was governed by thy hand.
text.KJV: For in the old time also when the proud gyants perished, the hope of the world gouerned by thy hand, escaped in a weake vessell, and left to all ages a seed of generation.
text.NABRE: For of old, when the proud giants were being destroyed, the hope of the universe, who took refuge on a raft, left to the world a future for the human family, under the guidance of your hand.

text.DRB: For blessed is the wood, by the which justice is made.
text.KJV: For blessed is the wood, whereby righteousnesse commeth.
text.NABRE: For blest is the wood through which righteousness comes about;

text.DRB: But the idol that is made by hands, cursed is both it, and he that made it: because he indeed wrought it: and the same being frail, was called God.
text.KJV: But that which is made with hands, is cursed, aswell it, as hee that made it: he, because he made it, and it, because being corruptible it was called God.
text.NABRE: but the handmade idol is accursed, and its maker as well: he for having produced it, and the corruptible thing, because it was termed a god.

text.DRB: But to God the impious and his impiety are odious alike.
text.KJV: For the vngodly and his vngodlines are both alike hatefull vnto God.
text.NABRE: Equally odious to God are the evildoer and the evil deed;

text.DRB: For that which is made, with him that made it, shall suffer torments.
text.KJV: For that which is made, shall bee punished together with him that made it.
text.NABRE: and the thing made will be punished with its maker.

text.DRB: For this cause also in the idol of the nations there shall be no respect: because the creatures of God were made to hatred, and for tentation to the souls of men, and for a snare to the feet of the unwise.
text.KJV: Therfore euen vpon the idoles of the Gentiles shall there be a visitation: because in the creature of God they are become an abomination and stumbling blocks to the soules of men, and a snare to the feet of the vnwise.
text.NABRE: Therefore upon even the idols of the nations shall a judgment come, since they became abominable among God’s works, Snares for human souls and a trap for the feet of the senseless.

text.KJV: For the deuising of idoles was the beginning of spiritual fornication, and the inuention of them the corruption of life.
text.NABRE: The Origin and Evils of Idolatry For the source of wantonness is the devising of idols; and their invention, a corruption of life.
text.NRSV: For the idea of making idols was the beginning of fornication, and the invention of them was the corruption of life.
text.DRB: For the beginning of fornication is the devising of idols: and the inventing of them is the corruption of life.

text.KJV: For neither were they from the beginning, neither shall they be for euer.
text.NABRE: For in the beginning they were not, nor can they ever continue;
text.NRSV: for they did not exist from the beginning, nor will they last forever.
text.DRB: For neither were they from the beginning, neither shall they be for ever.
For this vanity of men came into the world: and therefore there is found a short end of them.
text.KJV: For by the vaine glory of men they entred into the world, and therefore shall they come shortly to an end.
text.NABRE: for from human emptiness they came into the world, and therefore a sudden end is devised for them.

text.DRB: For the father being sorrowful with bitter mourning, made unto himself the image of his son quickly taken away: and him, that then was a dead man, now he began to worship as God, and appointed holy things and sacrifices among his servants.
text.KJV: For a father afflicted with vntimely mourning, when he hath made an image of his childe soone taken away, now honoured him as a god, which was then a dead man, and deliuered to those that were vnder him, ceremonies and sacrifices.
text.NABRE: For a father, afflicted with untimely mourning, made an image of the child so quickly taken from him, And now honored as a god what once was dead and handed down to his household mysteries and sacrifices.

text.DRB: Afterward by succession of time, the wicked custom prevailing, this error was kept as a law, and things graven were worshipped by the commandment of Tyrants.
text.KJV: Thus in processe of time an vngodly custome growen strong, was kept as a law, and grauen images were worshipped by the commandements of kings,
text.NABRE: Then, in the course of time, the impious practice gained strength and was observed as law, and graven things were worshiped by royal decrees.

text.DRB: And those, whom openly men could not honour, for that they were far off, their figure being brought from a far, they made an evident image of the king, whom they would honour: that by their carefulness they might honour as present, him that was absent.
text.KJV: Whom men could not honour in presence, because they dwelt farre off, they tooke the counterfeit of his visage from farre, and made an expresse image of a king whom they honoured, to the end that by this their forwardnes, they might flatter him that was absent, as if he were present.
text.NABRE: People who lived so far away that they could not honor him in his presence copied the appearance of the distant king And made a public image of him they wished to honor, out of zeal to flatter the absent one as though present.

text.DRB: And to the worshipping of these, the excellent diligence also of the artificer, holp them forward, that were ignorant.
text.KJV: Also the singular diligence of the artificer did helpe to set forward the ignorant to more superstition.
text.NABRE: And to promote this observance among those to whom it was strange, the artisan’s ambition provided a stimulus.

text.DRB: For he willing to please him that entertained him, laboured by his art to fashion the similitude in better sort.
text.KJV: For he peraduenture willing to please one in authoritie, forced all his skill to make the resemblance of the best fashion.
text.NABRE: For he, perhaps in his determination to please the ruler, labored over the likeness to the best of his skill;

text.DRB: And the multitude of men carried away by the beauty of the work, him that a little before had been honoured as a man, now they esteemed for a God.
text.KJV: And so the multitude allured by the grace of the worke, tooke him now for a god, which a litle before was but honoured as a man.
text.NABRE: And the masses, drawn by the charm of the workmanship, soon took as an object of worship the one who shortly before was honored as a human being.

text.DRB: And this was the deceiving of man's life: because men serving either affection, or kings, gave the name that is not communicable, to stones and wood.
text.KJV: And this was an occasion to deceiue the world: for men seruing either calamitie or tyrannie, did ascribe vnto stones, and stockes, the incommunicable Name.
text.NABRE: And this became a snare for the world, that people enslaved to either grief or tyranny conferred the incommunicable Name on stones and wood.

text.DRB: And it was not sufficient that they erred about the knowledge of God, but also living in a great battle of ignorance, so many and so great evils they call peace.
text.KJV: Moreouer this was not enough for them, that they erred in the knowledge of God, but whereas they liued in the great warre of ignorance, those so great plagues called they peace.
text.NABRE: Then it was not enough for them to err in their knowledge of God; but even though they live in a great war resulting from ignorance, they call such evils peace.

text.DRB: For either sacrificing their children, or making obscure sacrifices, or having watches full of madness,
text.KJV: For whilest they slew their children in sacrifices, or vsed secret ceremonies, or made reuellings of strange rites
text.NABRE: For while they practice either child sacrifices or occult mysteries, or frenzied carousing in exotic rites,

text.DRB: they now neither keep life, nor marriage clean, but one killeth another by envy, or playing the adulterer maketh him sorrowful:
text.KJV: They kept neither liues nor mariages any longer vndefiled: but either one slew another traiterously, or grieued him by adulterie:
text.NABRE: They no longer respect either lives or purity of marriage; but they either waylay and kill each other, or aggrieve each other by adultery.

text.DRB: and all things are mingled together, blood, manslaughter, theft and fiction, corruption and infidelity, trouble and perjury, disquieting of the good,
text.KJV: So that there reigned in all men without exception, blood, manslaughter, theft, and dissimulation, corruption, vnfaithfulnesse, tumults, periurie,
text.NABRE: And all is confusion—blood and murder, theft and guile, corruption, faithlessness, turmoil, perjury,

text.DRB: forgetfulness of God, inquination of souls, immutation of nativity, inconstancy of marriage, disorder of adultery, and unchasteness.
text.KJV: Disquieting of good men, forgetfulnesse of good turnes, defiling of soules, changing of kinde, disorder in mariages, adulterie, and shameles vncleannesse.
text.NABRE: Disturbance of good people, neglect of gratitude, besmirching of souls, unnatural lust, disorder in marriage, adultery and shamelessness.

text.KJV: For the worshipping of idoles not to be named, is the beginning, the cause, and the end of all euill.
text.NABRE: For the worship of infamous idols is the reason and source and extreme of all evil.
text.NRSV: For the worship of idols not to be named is the beginning and cause and end of every evil.
text.DRB: For the worship of idols not to be named, is the cause of all evil, and the beginning and end.
For either when they rejoice, they are mad: or certes prophesy false things, or live unjustly, or quickly forswear themselves.
text.KJV: For either they are mad when they be merry, or prophesie lies, or liue vniustly, or else lightly forsweare themselues.
text.NABRE: For they either go mad with enjoyment, or prophesy lies, or live lawlessly or lightly perjure themselves.

text.DRB: For whilst they trust in idols, which are without soul, swearing amiss they hope not to be hurt.
text.KJV: For insomuch as their trust is in idoles which haue no life, though they sweare falsly, yet they looke not to bee hurt.
text.NABRE: For as their trust is in lifeless idols, they expect no harm when they have sworn falsely.

text.DRB: Two evil things therefore shall happen to them worthily, because they have thought evil of God, attending to idols, and have sworn unjustly, in guile contemning justice.
text.KJV: Howbeit for both causes shal they be iustly punished: both because they thought not well of God, giuing heed vnto idols, and also vniustly swore in deceit, despising holinesse.
text.NABRE: But on both counts justice shall overtake them: because they thought perversely of God by devoting themselves to idols, and because they deliberately swore false oaths, despising piety.

text.DRB: For it is not the power of them that are sworn by, but the punishment of them that sin goeth always through the transgression of the unjust.
text.KJV: For it is not the power of them by whom they sweare: but it is the iust vengeance of sinners, that punisheth alwayes the offence of the vngodly.
text.NABRE: For it is not the might of those by whom they swear, but the just retribution of sinners, that ever follows upon the transgression of the wicked.

text.KJV: But thou O God, art gracious and true: long suffering, and in mercy ordering all things.
text.NABRE: Chapter 15 But you, our God, are good and true, slow to anger, and governing all with mercy.
text.NRSV: But you, our God, are kind and true, patient, and ruling all things in mercy.
text.DRB: But o thou our God art sweet and true, patient, and disposing all things in mercy.

text.KJV: For if we sinne we are thine, knowing thy power: but we will not sinne, knowing that we are counted thine.
text.NABRE: For even if we sin, we are yours, and know your might; but we will not sin, knowing that we belong to you.
text.NRSV: For even if we sin we are yours, knowing your power; but we will not sin, because we know that you acknowledge us as yours.
text.DRB: For if we sin, we are thine, knowing thy greatness: and if we sin not, we know that we are counted with thee.

text.KJV: For to know thee is perfect righteousnesse: yea to know thy power is the roote of immortality.
text.NABRE: For to know you well is complete righteousness, and to know your might is the root of immortality.
text.NRSV: For to know you is complete righteousness, and to know your power is the root of immortality.
text.DRB: For to know thee, is absolute justice: and to know justice, and thy power, is the root of immortality.
For men's invention of evil art hath not brought us into error, nor the shadow of a picture being a labour without fruit, a shape graven by diverse colours,
text.KJV: For neither did the mischieuous inuention of men deceiue vs: nor an image spotted with diuers colours, the painters fruitlesse labour.
text.NABRE: For the evil creation of human fancy did not deceive us, nor the fruitless labor of painters, A form smeared with varied colors,

text.DRB: the sight whereof giveth concupiscence to the senseless, and he loveth the shape without life of a dead image.
text.KJV: The sight wherof entiseth fooles to lust after it, and so they desire the forme of a dead image that hath no breath.
text.NABRE: the sight of which arouses yearning in a fool, till he longs for the inanimate form of a dead image.

text.DRB: The lovers of evils are worthy to have their hope in such things, both they that make them, and that love, and that worship them.
text.KJV: Both they that make them, they that desire them, and they that worship them, are louers of euill things, and are worthy to haue such things to trust vpon.
text.NABRE: Lovers of evil things, and worthy of such hopes are they who make them and long for them and worship them.

text.DRB: Yea and the potter pressing soft earth, with labour fashioneth every vessel to our uses, and of the same clay maketh the vessels that are clean to use, and in like manner them, that are contrary to these: but what the use of these vessels is, the potter is judge.
text.KJV: For the potter tempering soft earth fashioneth, euery vessell with much labour for our seruice: yea of the same clay hee maketh both the vessels that serue for cleane vses: and likewise also all such as serue to the contrary: but what is the vse of either sort, the potter himselfe is the iudge.
text.NABRE: The Potter’s Clay Idols For the potter, laboriously working the soft earth, molds for our service each single article: He fashions out of the same clay both the vessels that serve for clean purposes and their opposites, all alike; As to what shall be the use of each vessel of either class the worker in clay is the judge.

text.DRB: And with vain labour he fashioneth a god of the same clay: he which a little before was made of earth, and a little after returneth back whence he was taken, being exacted the debt of the life which he had.
text.KJV: And employing his labours lewdly, he maketh a vaine God of the same clay, euen he which a little before was made of earth himselfe, and within a little while after returneth to the same out of the which he was taken: when his life which was lent him shall be demanded.
text.NABRE: With misspent toil he molds a meaningless god from the selfsame clay, though he himself shortly before was made from the earth, And is soon to go whence he was taken, when the life that was lent him is demanded back.

text.DRB: But his care is, not because he shall labour, nor because he hath a short life, but he contendeth with gold-smiths and silver-smiths: yea and he imitateth the copper-smiths, and counteth it a glory, because he maketh vain things.
text.KJV: Notwithstanding his care is, not that hee shall haue much labour, nor that his life is short: but striueth to excel goldsmiths, and siluersmiths, and endeuoureth to doe like the workers in brasse, and counteth it his glory to make counterfeit things.
text.NABRE: But his concern is not that he is to die nor that his span of life is brief; Rather, he vies with goldsmiths and silversmiths and emulates molders of bronze, and takes pride in fashioning counterfeits.

text.DRB: For his heart is ashes, and his hope vain earth, and his life viler than clay:
text.KJV: His heart is ashes, his hope is more vile then earth, and his life of lesse value then clay:
text.NABRE: Ashes his heart is! more worthless than earth is his hope, more ignoble than clay his life;

text.DRB: because he was ignorant who made him, and who inspired into him the soul which worketh, & who breathed into him the vital spirit.
text.KJV: Forasmuch as hee knew not his maker, and him that inspired into him an actiue soule, and breathed in a liuing spirit.
text.NABRE: Because he knew not the one who fashioned him, and breathed into him a quickening soul, and infused a vital spirit.

text.DRB: Yea and they esteemed our life to be a pastime, and the conversation of life made for a gain, & that we must get every way even of evil.
text.KJV: But they counted our life a pastime, & our time here a market for gaine: for, say they, we must be getting euery way, though it be by euil meanes.
text.NABRE: Instead, he esteemed our life a mere game, and our span of life a holiday for gain; “For one must,” says he, “make a profit in every way, be it even from evil.”

text.DRB: For he knoweth that he offendeth above all men, which of the matter of earth fashioneth frail vessels, and sculptiles.
text.KJV: For this man that of earthly matter maketh brickle vessels, and grauen images, knoweth himselfe to offend aboue all others.
text.NABRE: For more than anyone else he knows that he is sinning, when out of earthen stuff he creates fragile vessels and idols alike.

text.DRB: For all the unwise and unhappy above measure of the soul, proud are the enemies of thy people, and rule over them:
text.KJV: And all the enemies of thy people, that hold them in subiection are most foolish and are more miserable then very babes.
text.NABRE: But most stupid of all and worse than senseless in mind, are the enemies of your people who enslaved them.

text.DRB: because they have esteemed all the idols of the nations for gods, which neither have use of eyes to see, nor nostrils to take breath, nor ears to hear, nor fingers of the hands to handle, yea and their feet are slow to walk.
text.KJV: For they counted all the idoles of the heathen to be gods: which neither haue the vse of eyes to see, nor noses to draw breath, nor eares to heare, nor fingers of hands to handle, and as for their feete they are slow to goe.
text.NABRE: For they esteemed all the idols of the nations as gods, which cannot use their eyes to see, nor nostrils to breathe the air, Nor ears to hear, nor fingers on their hands for feeling; even their feet are useless to walk with.

text.DRB: For a man made them: and he that borrowed breath, the same fashioned them. For no man can make God like to himself.
text.KJV: For man made them, and he that borrowed his owne spirit fashioned them, but no man can make a god like vnto himselfe.
text.NABRE: For it was a mere human being who made them; one living on borrowed breath who fashioned them. For no one is able to fashion a god like himself;

text.DRB: For whereas himself is mortal, he maketh a dead thing with his wicked hands. For he is better than they, whom he worshippeth, because he indeed lived, though he were mortal, but they never.
text.KJV: For being mortall he worketh a dead thing with wicked hands: for hee himselfe is better then the things which he worshippeth: whereas he liued once, but they neuer.
text.NABRE: he is mortal, and what he makes with lawless hands is dead. For he is better than the things he worships; he at least lives, but never his idols.

text.DRB: But they worship also most miserable beasts: for the senseless things compared to these, are worse than they.
text.KJV: Yea they worshipped those beasts also that are most hatefull: for being compared together, some are worse then others.
text.NABRE: Second Example Resumed Besides, they worship the most loathsome beasts— as regards stupidity, these are worse than the rest,

text.DRB: Yea neither by sight can any man see good of these beasts, But they have fled from the praise of God, and from his blessing.
text.KJV: Neither are they beautifull, so much, as to bee desired in respect of beasts, but they went without the praise of God and his blessing.
text.NABRE: For beasts are neither good-looking nor desirable; they have escaped both the approval of God and his blessing.

text.DRB: For these things, and by the like to these, they have worthily suffered torments, and were destroyed by a multitude of beasts.
text.KJV: Therefore by the like were they punished worthily, and by the multitude of beasts tormented.
text.NABRE: Chapter 16 Therefore they were fittingly punished by similar creatures, and were tormented by a swarm of insects.

text.DRB: For the which torments thou didst well dispose of thy people, to whom thou gavest the desire of their delectation, a new taste, preparing them the quail for meat:
text.KJV: In stead of which punishment, dealing graciously with thine owne people thou preparedst for them meate of a strange taste: euen quailes to stirre vp their appetite:
text.NABRE: Instead of this punishment, you benefited your people with a novel dish, the delight they craved, by providing quail for their food,

text.DRB: that they indeed coveting meat, because of those things which were shewed and sent them, might be turned away even from necessary concupiscence. But they in short time being made needy, tasted a new meat.
text.KJV: To the end that they desiring food might for the ougly sight of the beasts sent among them, loath euen that which they must needs desire: but these suffering penury for a short space, might be made partakers of a strange taste.
text.NABRE: So that those others, when they desired food, should lose their appetite even for necessities, since the creatures sent to plague them were so loathsome, While these, after a brief period of privation, partook of a novel dish.

text.DRB: For it behoved that without excuse destruction should come upon them exercising tyranny: but to these only to shew how their enemies were destroyed.
text.KJV: For it was requisite, that vpon them excercising tyranny should come penury which they could not auoyde: but to these it should onely be shewed how their enemies were tormented.
text.NABRE: For inexorable want had to come upon those oppressors; but these needed only to be shown how their enemies were being tormented.

text.DRB: For when the cruel wrath of beasts came upon them, they were destroyed with the bitings of perverse serpents.
text.KJV: For when the horrible fiercenesse of beasts came vpon these, and they perished with the stings of crooked serpents, thy wrath endured not for euer.
text.NABRE: For when the dire venom of beasts came upon them and they were dying from the bite of crooked serpents, your anger endured not to the end.

text.DRB: Howbeit thy wrath endured not for ever, but for chastisement they were troubled a short time, having a sign of salvation for the remembrance of the commandment of thy law.
text.KJV: But they were troubled for a smal season that they might be admonished, hauing a signe of saluation, to put them in remembrance of the commandement of thy Law.
text.NABRE: But as a warning, for a short time they were terrorized, though they had a sign of salvation, to remind them of the precept of your law.

text.KJV: For hee that turned himselfe towards it, was not saued by the thing that he saw: but by thee that art the sauiour of all.
text.NABRE: For the one who turned toward it was saved, not by what was seen, but by you, the savior of all.
text.NRSV: For the one who turned toward it was saved, not by the thing that was beheld, but by you, the Savior of all.
text.DRB: For he that turned to it, was not healed by that which he saw, but by thee the Saviour of all:
and in this thou didst shew to our enemies, that thou art he which deliverest from all evil.
text.KJV: And in this thou madest thine enemies confesse, that it is thou who deliuerest from all euill:
text.NABRE: By this also you convinced our foes that you are the one who delivers from all evil.

text.DRB: For the bitings of locusts and flies killed them, and their was found no remedy for their life: because they were worthy to be destroyed by such things.
text.KJV: For them the bitings of grassehoppers and flies killed, neither was there found any remedy for their life: for they were worthy to bee punished by such.
text.NABRE: For the bites of locusts and of flies slew them, and no remedy was found to save their lives because they deserved to be punished by such means;

text.DRB: But neither the teeth of venomous dragons overcame thy children: for thy mercy coming healed them.
text.KJV: But thy sonnes, not the very teeth of venemous dragons ouercame: for thy mercy was euer by them, and healed them.
text.NABRE: But not even the fangs of poisonous reptiles overcame your children, for your mercy came forth and healed them.

text.DRB: For in memory of thy words they were examined, & were quickly saved, lest falling into deep oblivion they might not use thy help.
text.KJV: For they were pricked, that they should remember thy words, and were quickly saued, that not falling into deep forgetfulnesse, they might be continually mindefull of thy goodnesse.
text.NABRE: For as a reminder of your injunctions, they were stung, and swiftly they were saved, Lest they should fall into deep forgetfulness and become unresponsive to your beneficence.

text.KJV: For it was neither herbe, nor mollifying plaister that restored them to health: but thy word, O Lord, which healeth all things.
text.NABRE: For indeed, neither herb nor application cured them, but your all-healing word, O Lord!
text.NRSV: For neither herb nor poultice cured them, but it was your word, O Lord, that heals all people.
text.DRB: For neither herb, nor plaster healed them, but thy word, o Lord, which healeth all things.

text.KJV: For thou hast power of life and death: thou leadest to the gates of hell, and bringest vp againe.
text.NABRE: For you have dominion over life and death; you lead down to the gates of Hades and lead back.
text.NRSV: For you have power over life and death; you lead mortals down to the gates of Hades and back again.
text.DRB: For it is thou, o Lord, that hast power of life and death, and bringest down to the gates of death, and fetchest again:
but man certes killeth by malice, and when the spirit is gone forth, it shall not return, neither shall he call back the soul that is received:
text.KJV: A man indeed killeth through his malice: and the spirit when it is gone foorth returneth not; neither the soule receiued vp, commeth againe.
text.NABRE: Human beings, however, may kill another with malice, but they cannot bring back the departed spirit, or release the soul that death has confined.

text.DRB: but it is unpossible to escape thy hand.
text.KJV: But it is not possible to escape thine hand.
text.NABRE: Your hand no one can escape.

text.DRB: For the impious denying to know thee, have been scourged by the strength of thine arm, suffering persecution by strange waters, and hail, and rain, and consumed by fire.
text.KJV: For the vngodly that denied to know thee, were scourged by the strength of thine arme: with strange raines, hailes, and showers were they persecuted, that they could not auoyd, and through fire were they consumed.
text.NABRE: Third Example: A Rain of Manna for Israel Instead of the Plague of Storms For the wicked who refused to know you were punished by the might of your arm, Were pursued by unusual rains and hailstorms and unremitting downpours, and were consumed by fire.

text.DRB: For that which was marvelous in water, which extinguisheth all things, fire more prevailed: for the world is revenger of the just.
text.KJV: For, which is most to be wondered at, the fire had more force in the water that quencheth all things: for the world fighteth for the righteous.
text.NABRE: For against all expectation, in water which quenches everything, the fire grew more active; For the universe fights on behalf of the righteous.

text.DRB: For a certain time, the fire was mitigated, that the beasts which were sent to the impious might not be burnt, but that they seeing might know that by God's judgment they suffer punishment.
text.KJV: For sometimes the flame was mitigated, that it might not burne vp the beasts that were sent against the vngodly: but themselues might see and perceiue that they were persecuted with the iudgement of God.
text.NABRE: Then the flame was tempered so that the beasts that were sent upon the wicked might not be burnt up, but that these might see and know that they were struck by the judgment of God;

text.DRB: And at a certain time the fire above his power burnt in water on every side, that it might destroy the nation of a wicked land.
text.KJV: And at another time it burneth euen in the midst of water, aboue the power of fire, that it might destroy the fruits of an vniust land.
text.NABRE: And again, even in the water, fire blazed beyond its strength so as to consume the produce of the wicked land.

text.KJV: In stead whereof thou feddest thine owne people, with Angels food, and didst send them from heauen bread prepared without their labour, able to content euery mans delight, and agreeing to euery taste.
text.NABRE: Instead of this, you nourished your people with food of angels and furnished them bread from heaven, ready to hand, untoiled-for, endowed with all delights and conforming to every taste.
text.NRSV: Instead of these things you gave your people food of angels, and without their toil you supplied them from heaven with bread ready to eat, providing every pleasure and suited to every taste.
text.DRB: For the which things thou didst nourish thy people with the meat of Angels, and bread prepared thou gavest them from heaven without labour, having in it all delectation, and the sweetness of all taste.
For thy substance did shew thy sweetness which thou hast toward thy children, and serving every man's will, it was turned to that, that every man would.
text.KJV: For thy sustenance declared thy sweetnesse vnto thy children, and seruing to the appetite of the eater tempered it selfe to euery mans liking.
text.NABRE: For this substance of yours revealed your sweetness toward your children, and serving the desire of the one who received it, was changed to whatever flavor each one wished.

text.DRB: Yea snow and ice sustained the force of fire, and melted not: that they might know that fire burning in hail, and lightening in rain destroyed the fruits of the enemies.
text.KJV: But snow and yce endured the fire and melted not, that they might know that fire burning the haile, and sparkling in the raine, did destroy the fruits of the enemies.
text.NABRE: Yet snow and ice withstood fire and were not melted, so that they might know that their enemies’ fruits Were consumed by a fire that blazed in the hail and flashed lightning in the rain.

text.DRB: And this again, that the just might be nourished, it forgat also his own strength.
text.KJV: But this againe did euen forget his owne strength, that the righteous might be nourished.
text.NABRE: But this fire, again, in order that the righteous might be nourished, forgot even its proper strength;

text.DRB: For the creature serving thee the Creator, is fierce into torment against the unjust: and is made more gentle to do good for them, that trust in thee.
text.KJV: For the creature that serueth thee who art the maker, encreaseth his strength against the vnrighteous for their punishment, and abateth his strength for the benefit of such as put their trust in thee.
text.NABRE: For your creation, serving you, its maker, grows tense for punishment against the wicked, but is relaxed in benefit for those who trust in you.

text.DRB: For this cause even then being transformed into all things, they served thy grace the nurse of all, at their will that desired thee:
